speaker: interviewer
text: Eli Benyacar. This is tape seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Benyacar, has your belief in God or the way you practice your religion changed as a result of what you went through during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me put it this way. When I was at home, I didn't know too much. I mean, we observed all the holidays and everything else, but I never participate because I never learned how to read Hebrew. Or-- you know, this is one thing, but it has nothing to do. My family still observed all the religion-- all the holidays and all the kosher [INAUDIBLE], all the kosher we used to have at home. Everything was done. Now, to me, I didn't pay no part in being religious. But as far as I know, I was a little boy. And my father told me, today, or tomorrow is Yom Kippur. We've got to go to shul. I studied shul. Whatever holiday we had, I observed that. Like we used to have Pesach. We observed the eight days of Pesach. We do everything. What we do here today was something that I do with my grandchildren and my children here today is exactly what I used to do at home. But I never learned how to read. But the only thing I used to-- even today, I read in Spanish a few words here, a few [YIDDISH] from the Haggadah, but I do not know how to read.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as far as religious concern, I always wonder, where was God? I mean, I still-- I still call God when I'm in trouble. I always say, oh, God, help me, you know? But I'll come back when I was in concentration camp. Where was God? When my little sister, my mother and father, they took them to the oven. They were innocent people. If there was a God at those days, he shouldn't let them do those things. But here we go again. We pray-- we are-- they claim we are the chosen people. For what? To go to the ovens? To go to the crematoriums? And this is the chosen people that we are? And-- and I just-- I can take God, take it or leave it, you know? But I will not encourage my kids, God forbid. They've got to believe on that, because my biggest tradition is on having the kids on a Friday night and have our good Kiddush, and which everybody-- is my biggest pleasure. My biggest pleasure is when I see my grandson, Lee, gets up on top of the chair, stands up, and he says the Kiddush, like [YIDDISH] means like a homemade Kiddush, which I-- I didn't do this, you know? And when I was at home, I used to say the Shema every night before I went to bed. My father would wait for me, or I'd wait for my father, because I used to sleep in one bed with my father. And my little sister used to sleep in the bed with my mother. And the two brothers and the other sister is in a different room, you know? There were-- we were-- we had two rooms in our apartment, a living room and a kitchen. But the three of them slept in one room, and the four of us, we slept in the other room. But when I was at home, my father taught me to say the Shema every night, even I used to say the Shema when I even came to-- to Canada. Till-- 1950s. I used to say-- I used to have it on me, that, you know, I'm going to bed, you know? I used to say it in Spanish more than in Hebrew. I said it half in Hebrew. half in Spanish. And I enjoyed saying it. But in a way, I take it, and I leave it, you know? It's-- but in another way, I says, well, maybe God spare me, you know? You know, it's hard to-- I'm not a-- a fanatic Jew. I'm not a fanatic. I mean, I know I went through a lot. And sometimes, you go to shul, it helps clean up your system inside. It doesn't mean you have to-- I don't have to believe it. But as long as you go in, you express yourself, you know, you read the Torah, you know-- and like, for instance, my granddaughter at the bat mitzvah, she read the Torah. Like tears come in my eyes, you know? And it was lots of [NON-ENGLISH] lots of-- lot of pleasure for me to see those things that I accomplish all these years, you see? And-- Mr. Benycara, you have children, you have grandchildren. What would be your special message to them and to the next generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be faithful. To-- to work-- to work to a better life, not to-- not to be a copycat, to be independent, to be leaders. I was a leader. I always was a leader. And I told my sons and my whole family to be a leader, not to be a follower. If you are a follower, you are in the-- in the gutter, you know? And the main thing is to be a leader.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even when I came to this country here, and I used to be with all the Polish Jews and Hungarian Jews-- and I was the only Greek Jew in between them, which my Yiddish language was a half what I know today-- but I was a leader. I never follow them, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I will say today, we're going to Center Island, you know, for having a good time. They'll go with them. Or even today, I have another friend. We go to a restaurant. I will-- if you want to join me, you go wherever I want, because I know the food I will eat. But if I go to their restaurant, I'm a fussy eater. They can eat anything, so I don't go with them. If they want to-- if they want to join me, they can come wherever I say, or whatever. This means a leader.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my kids, I tell them to be a leader, not to be a follower, because it depends either-- when the kids grown up, if they grew up with, you know, bad people, they become people-- bad people. But I keep my family to watch who they're going with, you see? And so far, my grandchildren are young. But I know my kids are doing all right. My two boys were successful businesspeople. Boys were very, very successful. And my daughter is, [NON-ENGLISH] She's tremendous. She's a very good girl and a good mother. So nothing else that I can ask. I'd like--

speaker: interviewee
text: For a DP, for a newcomer who came to this country-- who came to this country with nothing. So I can't-- I can't complain. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you, very much, for allow us to interview you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's a pleasure. This will be a good souvenir for my grandchildren then when I'll be gone, which [INAUDIBLE]. Give us another 10, 15 years, none of us will be around. None of us will be around. And a movie like that, there will be a memory for them, even though might not believe everything they see. But at least one of their families said it, you know? So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pleasure. This picture that you see now is my little sister, Dora, and my second oldest brother, Solomon. And I believe was taken around 1936 or 1937. I'm not 100% sure. But that's where they are. This picture is my sister, Esther, which was about three years older than I was. And it was taken in 1943, which she perished in Birkenau. And I never saw her after that. This picture is of me, Eli Benyacar, taken in Italy in 1945, which I was 18 years old. This picture was taken in 1947 in Feldafing. The whole group here are the survivors from the concentration camp, which we were liberated together in Feldafing in Tutzing, in Feldafing. And I am-- the picture-- it-- and I am in this picture just behind-- in the second row, just behind the guy with the tie. I'm wearing a T-shirt. I don't wear a jacket. I'm not wearing a jacket. And the guy with the-- with the tie, his name is Ovadia, which he lives in Israel up till today. Some of them are still alive, and some, they are gone. This is the passport that I came to Canada in 1948. It was taken in Germany, and which I had a hard time getting it, which it cost me plenty. That's it. [LAUGHS] This is my second oldest brother, Solomon Benyacar, which he did survive. And he did pass away three years ago, in September. And he had-- he died of cancer, unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture taken in one of the affairs, which I'm not sure which one it is. It must be one of the bar mitzvahs or--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Toronto. Yeah. Yeah. Might be one of the bar mitvahs or one of the-- It has to be one of the bar mitzvahs. My name is Eli Benyacar. This is the result of a single boy coming in 1948 in Toronto, Canada. The outlook-- the outcome is as follows. On my right hand, I got my lovely wife, Ester. And on my left, I got my daughter-in-law, named Andrea. And next to her, is a little-- my little granddaughter. Her name, Marli. And next to him-- to her is my second son, Ian. On my extreme right, I have Catherine, and with her daughter, Yasmin, and my-- my first son, Michael, and his grand-- and my grandson, Ilan. On the bottom floor, in the-- I got as follows. So let me see your faces. My sons--

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son's son, Ariel. Then we talking to my other son's daughter, Showna. And then we come to my little guy, who says Kiddush, beautiful Kiddush on a Friday night. His name is Lee. And, of course, my bat mitzvah girl, which turned up 15 and spoiled, which is Ruthie Cadesky. And I love them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Your daughter's not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilan, do you want to say something here?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's three people missing on this picture, is my lovely daughter with her husband, which are in-- they are on a semi-holiday, since he's an accountant, and he finisned all the tax things in the end of April. They took a break for the weekend in New York. And of course, my other son, Eli, they have-- unfortunately, had a friend, which was sick in the hospital. And they went to visit him. This is the outcome, believe it or not. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilan, want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-uh. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought you were going to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: How about you, Showna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say I love you, Nono. Come on.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, everybody, let's say, I love you, Nono.

speaker: interviewee
text: We love you, Nona and Nono.

speaker: interviewee
text: That a boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilan? OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to say something? No?
